%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
  Modern LLM serving systems use a global scheduler to manage request queuing and route 
inference calls across backend instances. However, LLM-based Agent workloads form dynamic 
multi-step execution graphs, where inter-request dependencies, evolving execution progress, 
and cache locality must be considered jointly. These properties expose limitations in existing 
queuing and routing policies. In queuing, prioritizing workflows near completion can reduce 
end-to-end latency, but First-Come-First-Serve ignores execution progress, while static 
topology-aware strategies become unreliable when dynamic branches or loops invalidate runtime 
estimates. In routing, schedulers must balance KV cache reuse and global load distribution: 
round-robin sacrifices cache locality, whereas static session affinity causes hotspots during 
parallel fan-out and adapts poorly to load shifts.

  To address these challenges, we present RAGS, an RL-based Agent Global Scheduler that jointly 
co-designs queuing and routing. Its queuing layer, DynA, requires no predefined topology and assigns 
priorities using an online-estimated completion probability derived from runtime step distributions, 
augmented by an aging mechanism to prevent starvation. Its routing layer formulates request assignment as a Markov Decision 
Process and uses a Transformer-based policy trained through deep reinforcement learning. 
Through offline pretraining and online fine-tuning, the policy jointly optimizes KV cache 
reuse, load balance, and queue congestion. Extensive evaluations on diverse Agent workloads in a simulated LLM serving cluster show 
that RAGS reduces mean end-to-end latency by up to 5.2x over round-robin and by 3--4x over 
state-of-the-art baselines. Additional studies further 
confirm the complementary effects of both layers and strong generalizability across heterogeneous GPU deployments.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}

Large Language Models (LLMs) have rapidly evolved from standalone research artifacts into 
the computational backbone of diverse intelligent applications. Modern LLM serving systems 
rely heavily on a global scheduler to manage request queuing and route inference calls across 
backend instances~\cite{kwon2023efficient,yu2022orca}. However, these conventional schedulers 
are fundamentally designed for independent, single-turn requests, treating each inference call 
as an isolated scheduling unit.

LLM-based Agent systems inherently challenge this assumption. Applications such as multi-turn 
chatbots, autonomous code generation pipelines, and large-scale document processing workflows 
orchestrate multiple LLM calls into dynamic, multi-step execution graphs~\cite{yao2022react}. 
As these deployments scale to handle thousands of concurrent workflows, global scheduling 
must evolve beyond request-level efficiency. It must strictly account for workflow-level dependencies, 
dynamic execution progress, and the critical trade-off between KV cache locality and cluster-wide load 
balancing. Consequently, these structural properties expose severe limitations in existing global queuing 
and routing policies.

%%Classical scheduling strategies designed for stateless, single-turn inference 
%%fall short in these dynamic scenarios, as they rely on assumptions that Agent 
%%workloads inherently contradict. First, Agent workflows feature dynamic, nonlinear 
%%topologies, such as conditional branches and iterative repair loops~\cite{yao2022react}. Because 
%%actual execution paths and depths are resolved only at runtime, static graph-based 
%%priority estimation becomes ineffective. Second, requests generated across different 
%%stages of a single workflow are heterogeneous in input/output lengths and GPU 
%%memory footprints. Consequently, uniform scheduling treatment typically induces 
%%head-of-line blocking and resource imbalance. Third, adjacent calls within a workflow 
%%contain overlapping context prefixes. This presents KV cache reuse opportunities~\cite{kwon2023efficient,zheng2024sglang}, 
%%forcing a strict trade-off between cache locality and cluster-wide load balancing.

%%Existing approaches only partially address these scheduling challenges. On the queuing 
%%side, FCFS remains unaware of workflow progress, propagating queuing delays along dataflow 
%%edges. Topology-aware strategies like Ayo~\cite{tan2025towards} prioritize requests 
%%based on static residual stage counts. However, they become ineffective when dynamic 
%%branches and loops cause the estimated stage depth to deviate from the actual execution 
%%trajectory. On the routing front, Round-Robin policies achieve load balance at the expense 
%%of prefix-cache locality. Conversely, Session Affinity, as adopted by end-to-end systems 
%%such as Parrot~\cite{lin2024parrot}, maximizes cache reuse via static session binding. 
%%Yet, this approach induces hardware hotspots when parallel fan-out stages concentrate requests 
%%on a single instance, and it lacks the adaptability to handle dynamic load shifts, frequently 
%%resulting in cluster-wide load imbalances. Taken together, these limitations indicate that 
%%effective global scheduling for LLM Agent serving necessitates the co-design of queuing and routing 
%%algorithms, as neither tier can achieve robust performance in isolation.
On the queuing side, Agent workflows diverge fundamentally from conventional single-turn workloads in both 
structure and objective. Because these workflows frequently incorporate conditional branches and iterative 
repair loops, their true execution paths and depths are resolved only at runtime. Consequently, static topology-aware 
priority estimation becomes highly unreliable whenever branch outcomes or loop iterations deviate from prior expectations. 
Traditional First-Come-First-Serve (FCFS) queuing is equally inadequate; by remaining entirely oblivious to workflow progress, 
it often stalls near-completion tasks and amplifies end-to-end latency along dataflow dependencies. Furthermore, because 
requests from different workflow stages exhibit substantial variance in context lengths, generation lengths, and GPU memory 
footprints, applying a uniform scheduling treatment inevitably induces head-of-line blocking and resource imbalance.

On the routing side, Agent workloads force schedulers to navigate a strict trade-off between two competing objectives: 
cache locality and load balancing. Because adjacent inference calls within the same workflow typically share overlapping 
context prefixes, they present significant opportunities for KV cache reuse~\cite{kwon2023efficient,zheng2024sglang}. 
However, optimizing routing exclusively for cache locality frequently overloads specific backend instances, particularly 
during parallel fan-out stages. While conventional Round-Robin routing achieves uniform global load distribution, it 
completely sacrifices this prefix cache locality. Conversely, static session affinity, as adopted by systems like 
Parrot~\cite{lin2024parrot}, maximizes cache reuse via fixed instance binding but inevitably triggers hardware hotspots 
and struggles to adapt to dynamic runtime load shifts.

In response to these challenges, we present \textbf{RAGS} (\underline{R}L-based \underline{A}gent 
\underline{G}lobal \underline{S}cheduler), a co-designed global scheduling framework that 
addresses both queuing and routing dimensions in a coordinated manner. The queuing layer, \textbf{DynA}, 
determines request priorities using exclusively runtime-observable signals (specifically, online-estimated completion-round distributions, 
accumulated workflow latencies, and per-request queue ages), operating independently of any 
predefined workflow topology. Concurrently, the routing layer employs a Reinforcement Learning 
(RL) policy that models request-to-instance assignment as a Markov Decision Process. By training 
a Transformer-based network via Double-DQN, it jointly optimizes KV cache reuse, cluster load balance, 
and queue congestion, ensuring continuous adaptability through offline pretraining coupled with online 
fine-tuning.

The main contributions of this paper are as follows.
\begin{itemize}
  \item We propose an adaptive queuing algorithm that estimates the overall completion 
        probability of active workflows using statistics gathered at runtime. 
        By prioritizing individual requests based on the execution progress of their 
        parent workflows, this approach effectively mitigates head-of-line blocking among 
        concurrent tasks.

  \item To the best of our knowledge, RAGS is the first to apply reinforcement learning 
        to global routing in LLM Agent serving. By jointly encoding heterogeneous cluster features, 
        including KV cache states, load distributions, and GPU hardware configurations, 
        the RL policy realizes an adaptive, multi-objective routing strategy that strictly 
        outperforms traditional static heuristics.

  \item Evaluations on a simulated LLM serving cluster demonstrate that RAGS reduces mean 
        end-to-end workflow latency by up to \textbf{5.2$\times$} compared to a normal  
        Round-Robin baseline, and by \textbf{3--4$\times$} against Parrot and Ayo. Furthermore, 
        ablation studies and experiments on heterogeneous GPU clusters validate the individual 
        effectiveness and hardware adaptability of the co-designed queuing and routing modules.
\end{itemize}

\section{Background and Problem Analysis}
\label{sec:background-and-problem-analysis}

Conventionally, LLM serving systems employ a layered scheduling architecture in which 
the global scheduler manages two interconnected operations: (1) determining 
the execution priority of pending requests, and (2) routing each request to an 
appropriate backend instance. This section characterizes the distinctive structural 
properties of LLM Agent workloads to demonstrate why conventional request-level 
heuristics prove inadequate for these tasks. By exposing these systemic limitations, 
we motivate the necessity of the comprehensive global scheduling framework detailed 
in Section~\ref{sec:system-design}, which systematically addresses both the queuing 
and routing dimensions.

\subsection{Characteristics of LLM Agent Workflows}
\label{subsec:agent-workflow}

To systematically characterize LLM Agent workloads, we surveyed mainstream open-source projects 
on GitHub and analyzed representative paradigms, including chatbots, code generation, and document 
summarization. As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:agent-dag}, a typical Agent workflow 
can be abstracted as a directed graph. In this structural model, each node represents 
an individual LLM inference call or tool invocation, while each edge encodes a specific 
dataflow or control dependency. Based on this analysis, we identify three distinctive 
system-level traits inherent to Agent workloads.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  % 倒三角形：上排 (a)(b)，下排 (c) 居中
  \begin{subfigure}[c]{0.48\linewidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/2.1.a.png}
    \caption{Chatbot.}
    \label{fig:agent-dag-a}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[c]{0.48\linewidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/2.1.b.png}
    \caption{Map-Reduce document summarization.}
    \label{fig:agent-dag-b}
  \end{subfigure}

  \vspace{0.5cm}

  \null\hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[b]{0.48\linewidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/2.1.c.png}
    \caption{Code generation.}
    \label{fig:agent-dag-c}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill\null
  \caption{Representative LLM Agent workflow structures.}
  \label{fig:agent-dag}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Dynamic, nonlinear topologies with mixed concurrency.}
Agent workflows incorporate conditional branches, feedback loops, and interleaved
sequential and parallel stages (e.g., iterative repair cycles and Map-phase fan-outs).
Consequently, these features result in dynamically changing execution paths, rendering 
static graph-based scheduling approaches ineffective.

\textbf{Heterogeneous inference costs across workflow nodes.}
Nodes serving different roles (such as routing, generation, and quality review) emit 
requests with varying prompt lengths, output lengths, and execution latencies. 
Because these resource requirements can differ by over $25\times$ within a single 
application~\cite{chen2025kairos}, scheduling decisions must be strictly cost-aware.

\textbf{Deep context sharing and KV cache affinity.}
Adjacent calls within the same workflow contain overlapping context prefixes, 
presenting KV cache reuse opportunities. However, affinity-driven routing inherently 
concentrates requests and induces node-level hotspots, forcing a strict trade-off 
between cache locality and cluster-wide load balancing.

Taken together, these architectural traits inherently contradict the classical assumption 
of independent, homogeneous requests. The following two subsections examine the concrete 
limitations that arise when standard queuing and routing policies are applied to Agent 
workloads.

\subsection{Limitations of Existing Queuing Algorithms}
\label{subsec:queue-challenges}

Conventional LLM serving paradigms model requests as independent, single-turn calls, 
where FCFS queuing is sufficient for fairness. Agent workloads, 
however, comprise interdependent requests that introduce three fundamental scheduling 
challenges.

\noindent\textbf{(1) Head-of-line blocking from workflow progress heterogeneity.}
Concurrent Agent requests exhibit varying execution progress, meaning the system 
simultaneously processes initial LLM calls and near-terminal tasks. Because FCFS 
is progress-agnostic, it frequently stalls near-completion requests behind newly 
admitted early-stage tasks, directly inflating end-to-end workflow latencies.

\noindent\textbf{(2) Cascading amplification and accumulation of queuing delays.}
Queuing delays experienced at any individual node propagate along dataflow dependencies. 
Furthermore, because FCFS ranks requests solely by arrival time, it ignores accumulated 
workflow-level latencies. Consequently, tail performance degrades as delays compound 
across successive stages.

\noindent\textbf{(3) Inability to handle dynamic workflow topologies.}
Topology-aware strategies like Ayo~\cite{tan2025towards} prioritize tasks with fewer 
remaining stages based on a static workflow graph. However, when dynamic branches or 
loops execute at runtime, the statically estimated residual stage counts deviate from 
actual execution paths, causing tasks within active loops to be improperly 
under-prioritized.

Collectively, these limitations necessitate an adaptive queuing policy that tracks 
actual workflow progress and accumulated latencies independent of static topologies.

\subsection{Limitations of Existing Routing Strategies}
\label{subsec:routing-challenges}
\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/2.3.pdf}
  \caption{RR versus Session Affinity under parallel and sequential
    document-summarization Agent workflows.}
  \Description{Placeholder for the figure comparing Round-Robin and Session
    Affinity across parallel and sequential workflow shapes.}
  \label{fig:routing-motivation}
\end{figure}

Conventional global routing mechanisms for LLM serving typically utilize 
Round-Robin (RR) to distribute requests sequentially for load balancing, 
or Session Affinity (SA) to bind session requests to a fixed instance for 
maximizing KV cache reuse. However, both strategies exhibit structural 
limitations when processing Agent workloads.

\textbf{RR ignores request heterogeneity.} 
Because requests from distinct functional nodes exhibit diverse context 
lengths and GPU memory footprints, RR's uniform routing often concentrates 
memory-intensive tasks on a single instance. This concentration saturates 
GPU memory and triggers request preemptions. Consequently, preempted tasks 
incur re-queuing and recomputation overheads, directly degrading overall 
system throughput.

\textbf{SA mishandles intra-workflow concurrency.} 
During parallel execution stages (e.g., the Map phase in document 
summarization), workflows emit numerous concurrent subtask requests. 
By binding all these requests to a single instance, SA creates local 
hardware hotspots while leaving other instances underutilized, thereby 
compromising cluster-wide load balancing.

As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:routing-motivation}, RR achieves 
lower latency on parallel workflows by exploiting 
cluster-wide concurrency, whereas SA outperforms RR on sequential 
workflows by preserving KV cache locality across stages. 
Because neither policy dominates universally, the optimal routing choice 
depends jointly on execution topologies, temporal dependencies, and 
real-time cache states.

To address these limitations, recent systems incorporate richer heuristics, 
such as multi-objective linear weighting. 
However, LLM cluster states evolve continuously: request arrival patterns, 
instance queue lengths, and memory utilization fluctuate rapidly. Furthermore, 
each routing decision reshapes the environment encountered by subsequent 
requests. Static, fixed-weight heuristics lack the agility to capture these 
complex state transitions or to optimize the trade-off between immediate 
scheduling gains and long-horizon throughput.

Collectively, the isolated failures of existing queuing and routing algorithms 
necessitate the holistic global scheduling framework detailed in 
Section~\ref{sec:system-design}.

\section{Algorithm Design}\label{sec:system-design}

\subsection{System Architecture}
\label{subsec:rags-arch}

Figure~\ref{fig:rags-arch} illustrates the overall system architecture 
and request processing pipeline of RAGS. The system frontend accepts 
heterogeneous Agent workflows and forwards all LLM inference requests 
to the global scheduler for centralized management. Within the scheduler, 
requests first enter the global queue, where the adaptive queuing module, 
\textbf{DynA}, calculates priorities and reorders pending tasks. 
Subsequently, the RL-driven routing policy asynchronously assigns these 
requests to target instances by evaluating queue characteristics alongside 
cluster states. Following inference completion, the system triggers 
subsequent workflow stages. Concurrently, backend instances continuously 
report load metrics back to the scheduler to maintain an 
accurate environmental state. Once the workflow concludes, the system returns 
the final result to the user.
\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/3.1.pdf}
  \caption{RAGS system architecture.}
  \label{fig:rags-arch}
\end{figure}

%Based on this architecture, we elaborate on the two core
%components, namely the queuing algorithm and the routing algorithm,
%in the following subsections.
Building upon this architectural overview, the following subsections detail 
the two core scheduling components: the queuing algorithm and the routing policy.

\subsection{DynA: Dynamic-Aware Queuing Algorithm}\label{sec:queue-algorithm}
Due to the dynamic branching and autoregressive uncertainty inherent in Agent 
workflows, traditional queuing policies that rely on static topologies 
prove inadequate. These methods assume a priori knowledge of the workflow structure, 
a condition rarely satisfied in actual deployments.

To overcome this reliance, we introduce \textbf{DynA} 
(\underline{Dyn}amic-\underline{A}ware Queuing). The core principle 
of DynA is to derive all priority decisions exclusively from runtime-observable 
signals, including online-estimated completion-round distributions, accumulated 
workflow latencies, and per-request queue ages. By doing so, it operates entirely 
independently of predefined workflow topologies. Specifically, the algorithm 
functions across two dimensions: inter-Agent priority ordering governed 
by actual workflow progress, and intra-Agent request ordering driven by 
accumulated latencies.

\subsubsection{Progress-Driven Inter-Agent Prioritization}
\label{subsec:inter-agent}

To distinguish the execution progress of varying Agent types and prioritize 
requests nearing completion, DynA introduces an online completion-round distribution 
mechanism. This approach evaluates macroscopic workflow progress without 
requiring any a priori knowledge of the underlying topology.

Let $F_a(n)$ denote the empirical CDF of completion rounds for Agent
type $a$. Specifically, this defines the probability that the total 
number of LLM inference calls within a completed workflow instance of 
this type does not exceed $n$. The system maintains this distribution 
dynamically by aggregating statistics from finished workflows at runtime, 
effectively bypassing the need for static structural assumptions.

Specifically, upon the completion of any Agent workflow of type $a$, the
system records its completion round count $n_j$ and updates the empirical CDF $F_a(n)$
using an Exponential Moving Average (EMA):
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:ema-update}
  F_a^{(t+1)}(n) = (1-\lambda)\cdot F_a^{(t)}(n) + \lambda\cdot\mathbb{1}[n_j \le n],
\end{equation}
where $\lambda \in (0,1)$ denotes the smoothing coefficient and
$\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function. By weighting recent 
observations more heavily, this EMA mechanism allows $F_a(n)$ to continuously 
adapt to workload shifts while gracefully decaying the influence of historical data.

For Agent types lacking historical records, the system initializes
$F_a$ with a uniform prior over the interval $\left[1, N_{\max}\right]$:
\[
  F_a(n) = \begin{cases}
    0,            & n < 1 \\
    n/N_{\max},   & 1 \le n \le N_{\max} \\
    1,            & n > N_{\max}
  \end{cases}.
\]
During online execution, the EMA mechanism continuously drives this prior toward 
the actual workload distribution.

Building upon this dynamically updated CDF, when the scheduler evaluates a request from
Agent type $a$ that has already completed $k$ LLM calls,
it calculates the conditional completion probability $\hat{P}_{a,k}$. 
This metric quantifies the likelihood that the workflow will terminate 
within the next $\Delta$ steps, given that $k$ steps have already been executed:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:conditional-prob}
  \hat{P}_{a,k} = P(k \le N_a \le k{+}\Delta \mid N_a \ge k)
                = \frac{F_a(k{+}\Delta) - F_a(k{-}1)}{1 - F_a(k{-}1)},
\end{equation}
where $N_a$ denotes the random variable representing total LLM calls for
Agent type $a$, and $\Delta$ defines a look-ahead window (default
$\Delta{=}1$). Because a larger $\hat{P}_{a,k}$ signifies an imminent completion, 
DynA elevates the scheduling priority of such requests. This design effectively 
approximates the Shortest Job First (SJF) policy, operating independent 
of predefined workflow topologies.

\subsubsection{Intra-Agent Ordering via Cumulative Latency}
\label{subsec:intra-agent}

To resolve scheduling ties among concurrent requests of the same Agent 
type and completion round, DynA applies an intra-Agent ordering strategy 
driven by workflow start times. Let
$t_r^{\text{start}}$ denote the initial invocation time of the workflow containing 
request $r$. The system prioritizes requests with earlier
$t_r^{\text{start}}$ values, compensating requests that have 
accumulated greater end-to-end delays.

To prevent the starvation of long-horizon workflows under this priority scheme, 
DynA incorporates an aging mechanism based on per-request wait times. 
Let $\tau_r = t_{\text{current}} - t_r^{\text{arrive}}$ define the current queuing 
delay for request $r$. Once $\tau_r$ exceeds a predefined maximum tolerance 
threshold $\tau_{\max}$, the system triggers a proportional priority boost:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:age-boost}
  \text{Boost}(r) = \max\!\left(0,\;\frac{\tau_r - \tau_{\max}}{\tau_{\max}}\right).
\end{equation}

Integrating the conditional completion probability with this aging mechanism, 
DynA formulates a unified priority score for each queued request $r$:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:priority-score}
  \text{Score}(r) = \hat{P}_{a_r, k_r} + \eta \cdot \text{Boost}(r),
\end{equation}
where $a_r$ identifies the Agent type of $r$, $k_r$ tracks the executed LLM calls 
for its workflow instance, and $\eta$ serves as the aging weight hyperparameter. 
Requests with higher scores are scheduled first, with any remaining ties broken 
by $t_r^{\text{start}}$. Algorithm~\ref{alg:dyna} summarizes the complete DynA policy.

\begin{algorithm}
  \caption{Dynamic-Aware Queuing Algorithm (DynA)}
  \label{alg:dyna}
  \begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \REQUIRE Request queue $Q$; online completion-round distributions
             $\{F_a\}$; anti-starvation threshold $\tau_{\max}$;
             weight $\eta$
    \ENSURE  Next request to route $r^*$
    \FOR{each request $r \in Q$}
      \STATE Retrieve Agent type $a_r$ and completed call count $k_r$
      \IF{$F_{a_r}$ is not yet initialized}
        \STATE $F_{a_r}(n)\leftarrow \min(n,N_{\max})/N_{\max}$
               \quad\textit{// uniform prior}
      \ENDIF
      \STATE $\hat{P}_{a_r,k_r}\leftarrow
             \dfrac{F_{a_r}(k_r{+}\Delta)-F_{a_r}(k_r{-}1)}
                   {1-F_{a_r}(k_r{-}1)}$
      \STATE $\tau_r \leftarrow t_{\text{current}} - t_r^{\text{arrive}}$
      \STATE $\text{Boost}(r)\leftarrow
             \max\!\left(0,\;\dfrac{\tau_r-\tau_{\max}}{\tau_{\max}}\right)$
      \STATE $\text{Score}(r)\leftarrow
             \hat{P}_{a_r,k_r} + \eta\cdot\text{Boost}(r)$
    \ENDFOR
    \STATE Sort all requests in $Q$ by
           $\bigl(\text{Score}(r)\;\text{desc},\;
                  t_r^{\text{start}}\;\text{asc}\bigr)$
    \STATE $r^*\leftarrow$ first request in $Q$
    \RETURN $r^*$
  \end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection{RL-based Routing Algorithm}
\label{sec:rl-routing}

\subsubsection{Problem Formulation and MDP Definition}
\label{subsec:mdp}

We formalize the global scheduler's routing process as a Markov Decision Process 
(MDP) and employ reinforcement learning to derive an optimal routing policy.

Consider a service system comprising $K$ LLM instances, each 
deploying model $M$ on associated GPU hardware $G$. During 
continuous system operation, $N$ Agent workflows arrive dynamically. 
At any decision step $t$, let $Q_t$ denote the set of pending requests 
within the global scheduler. For each request $q_{i:k}$ (representing 
the $k$-th inference call of workflow $w_i$), the scheduler must assign 
a target instance $a_t \in \{1, \dots, K\}$. The fundamental optimization 
objective is to minimize the average end-to-end latency across all $N$ workflows:
\begin{equation}
  \min_{a_t}\;\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} l_i,
\end{equation}
where $l_i$ defines the actual end-to-end completion time of workflow $w_i$.

Since $Q_t$ often contains multiple concurrent requests, a centralized joint 
routing decision would necessitate an action space dimension of $O(|Q_t|\!\times\!K)$. 
Such a high-dimensional output space significantly impedes RL training efficiency 
and convergence. To resolve this, we adopt a sequential decision-making paradigm: 
the agent evaluates and routes pending requests individually.

Formally, the MDP operates as follows. At every interval $\Delta t$, 
the global scheduler sequentially processes all requests residing in 
the waiting queue. For the $j$-th request $r_j$ during time step $t$, 
the agent observes the localized state $s_{t:j}$ and executes a discrete 
action $a_t^j \in \{1,\dots,K\}$, effectively routing $r_j$ to the corresponding 
LLM instance.

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/3.2.png}
  \caption{Markov Decision Process for global routing.}
  \Description{Placeholder for the MDP diagram.}
  \label{fig:chap4-mdp}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{State Space}

The state $s_t$ encodes the current global cluster state, comprising
features from the pending request queue and the real-time load of each LLM instance

\textbf{Request state.} The request feature matrix $\mathbf{X}_t^{req}$ 
consists of four distinct vectors:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\vec{p}$ is the input length vector, where $p_i$ denotes the input token count for the $i$-th queued request.
\item $\vec{d}$ is the estimated output length vector, where $d_i$ represents the predicted generation length of the $i$-th request.
\item $\vec{u}$ is the concurrency indicator vector, where $u_i \in \{0, 1\}$ flags whether the $i$-th request belongs to a parallel workflow stage.
\item $\vec{\kappa}$ is the workflow type vector, where $\kappa_i$ identifies the Agent application class of the $i$-th request.
\end{itemize}

To determine the estimated output length $d_i$, we adopt the methodology 
from~\cite{jin2023s}, predicting discrete length intervals rather than exact 
token counts. This coarse-grained approximation is highly effective because 
the routing MDP relies strictly on relative interval magnitudes. Empirical 
evaluations confirm that this interval prediction achieves approximately 70\% accuracy.

\textbf{Cluster state.} The instance feature matrix $\mathbf{X}_t^{inst}$ is constructed by 
concatenating individual vectors of all LLM instances. Specifically, 
the runtime state of each backend instance is characterized by the following components:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\vec{m}$ and $\vec{g}$: One-hot encoded vectors identifying the deployed model 
                    architecture and the specific GPU hardware configuration, respectively.
  \item $\vec{wq}$: A load histogram of the waiting queue, generated by binning input lengths 
                    using a configurable step size.
  \item $\vec{rq}$: A load histogram of active execution queues. Here, requests in 
                    the prefill phase are binned by input length, while those in the 
                    decode phase are binned by estimated output length.
  \item $c$: The GPU memory utilization, defined as the ratio of allocated KV 
          cache blocks to total block capacity.
  \item $s$: The expected prefix cache reuse rate for the pending request, 
            quantified by the fraction of reusable tokens.
  \item $b$: The estimated batch execution latency, tracked via a 
            sliding window average of recent iteration times.
\end{itemize}

To ensure RL training stability, all extracted features are strictly normalized. 
The comprehensive MDP state representation is thus formulated as 
$s_t = [\mathbf{X}_t^{req} \,\|\, \mathbf{X}_t^{inst}]$, where $\,\|\,$ 
denotes the concatenation operator.

\subsubsection{Reward Function}

Using end-to-end workflow latency directly as a reward signal
introduces severe reward sparsity and credit assignment challenges, 
given the asynchronous nature of LLM inference. To circumvent this,
we design a composite immediate reward that provides dense feedback
after every routing decision:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:routing-reward}
  r_t = \lambda_k\cdot r_k + \lambda_{imb}\cdot r_{imb}
       + \lambda_p\cdot r_p + \lambda_T\cdot T(x_{t,i}^{inst}),
\end{equation}
where the individual components are defined as follows:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $r_k$: \emph{Prefix cache reuse reward}, representing the expected
        KV cache hit rate to encourage a lower Time-to-First-Token (TTFT).
  \item $r_{imb}$: \emph{Load-imbalance penalty}, quantifying the routing 
        decision's impact on global load uniformity.
  \item $r_p$: \emph{Queue penalty}, reflecting the target instance's waiting queue 
        length to discourage routing to overloaded nodes.
  \item $T(x_{t,i}^{inst})$: \emph{Throughput estimate}, denoting the
        expected token throughput of the selected instance given its current state.
\end{itemize}

To compute the load imbalance penalty ($r_{imb} = -S_{imb}$), we first 
estimate the total load $L_i$ of instance $i$ by consolidating 
its active ($\vec{rq}$) and waiting ($\vec{wq}$) queue distributions:
\begin{equation}
  L_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{bin}} C_j \cdot (N_{run,j} + N_{pend,j}),
\end{equation}
where $C_j$ is the center length of bin $j$, while $N_{\cdot,j}$ is the
count of requests within that bin. The normalized imbalance score $S_{imb}$ 
is then calculated as:
\begin{equation}
  S_{imb} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K}|L_i - \bar{L}|}
                 {\sum_{i=1}^{K} L_i + \epsilon},
\end{equation}
where $\bar{L} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_i L_i$ acts as the average cluster load, 
and $\epsilon$ ensures numerical stability.

For the throughput term $T(x_{t,i}^{inst})$, we employ a lightweight neural 
surrogate model trained offline on historical routing trajectories, which achieves 
a relative prediction error of approximately 10\%.

\subsubsection{Policy Network Architecture}
\label{subsec:policy-net}

To capture complex temporal dependencies and heterogeneous instance
features, we introduce a Transformer-based~\cite{vaswani2017attention} scheduling network that
jointly encodes the target request, the waiting queue context,
and the runtime state of each instance.
Figure~\ref{fig:routing-policy-net} illustrates the network topology.
\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/3.3.pdf}
  \caption{Routing policy network architecture.}
  %\Description{Placeholder for the policy network architecture diagram.}
  \label{fig:routing-policy-net}
\end{figure}

The input layer comprises three feature subspaces: the 
target request $\mathbf{x}^{target}$, a look-ahead waiting queue
$\{\mathbf{x}^q_i\}_{i=1}^H$ of length $H$, and the dynamic states
of $K$ LLM instances $\{\mathbf{x}^{inst}_j\}_{j=1}^K$.
Each feature vector is first mapped to a common hidden dimension
$d_h$ via a linear projection, and subsequently augmented with learned 
type embeddings to distinguish target, queue, and instance tokens 
($\text{type}\in\{0,1,2\}$). 
The resulting sequence is processed by $L$ Transformer encoder layers
to capture cross-token interactions.
At the output layer, we extract the $K$ instance-token
representations $\{\mathbf{z}_{inst,j}\}_{j=1}^K$ and map each to an
action-value score via a shared parameter $Q$-value head:
\begin{equation}
  Q(s,a_j) = \mathbf{W}_Q\cdot\text{ReLU}
             \!\left(\mathbf{W}_H\mathbf{z}_{inst,j}+\mathbf{b}_H\right)+b_Q.
\end{equation}
The final output $\mathbf{Q}\in\mathbb{R}^K$ denotes the expected
cumulative return for routing the current request to each
instance.

\subsubsection{Reinforcement Learning Training}
\label{sec:training}

We adopt a two-phase training paradigm that combines offline pretraining 
with online fine-tuning.

\textbf{Offline Pretraining.}
We construct a data-driven offline simulation environment by replaying
diverse real production workload traces at various load intensities, 
ensuring robust policy generalization.

To effectively navigate the discrete action space, we employ the
Double-DQN algorithm~\cite{van2016deep}, which decouples
target action selection from value estimation to prevent Q-value
overestimation. 
Furthermore, we replace the standard MSE loss with Huber Loss, which
applies linear penalty to large TD errors, significantly improving
training stability under heterogeneous request sequences.

\textbf{Online Fine-tuning.}
Because static policies inevitably degrade in highly dynamic production environments, 
we deploy an online fine-tuning mechanism to iteratively optimize the agent. The global 
scheduler maintains a continuous feedback loop by logging runtime transitions into an 
experience replay buffer. Once sufficient samples are accumulated, the system initiates 
parameter updates at a low learning rate. To guarantee Quality of Service (QoS) stability 
during this live adaptation, 
we strictly bound the $\epsilon$-greedy exploration rate and employ momentum-based soft 
updates for the target network, smoothing potential Q-value oscillations caused by workload 
distribution shifts.

\section{Implementation}\label{sec:implementation}

To support RL agent training and system evaluation, we build our testbed upon 
Vidur~\cite{agrawal2024vidur}, an open-source LLM inference simulator. 
Vidur natively supports heterogeneous GPUs (e.g., A100, H100) and provides 
built-in benchmarks for mainstream models like Llama and Qwen, enabling rapid 
validation of scheduling algorithms across diverse configurations.

However, native Vidur primarily targets single-turn LLM requests. 
It lacks the capability to simulate the multi-step interaction dependencies 
inherent in Agent workflows, nor does it support prefix-cache reuse. 
To address these limitations, we augment Vidur's core engine along two dimensions:

\subsection{KV Cache Reuse}

To enable KV cache reuse within the LLM engine (i.e., the local scheduler), 
we implement a \texttt{tree\_cache} module atop Vidur's native \texttt{memory\_pool}. 
This module comprises two core components: a \emph{Block Manager} responsible for 
the allocation and reclamation of memory blocks, and a \emph{Local Prefix Cache Tree} 
that maintains the prefix-mapping relationships of KV caches within each LLM instance.

Unlike sglang's single-level prefix tree, our design maintains both a
per-instance local tree and a cluster-wide global tree augmented with
replica mappings to enable cross-instance KV cache reuse.
As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:tree-cache-total}, different request
prefixes branch from the root node. Each node stores the token
sequence, a reference count (\texttt{ref}) for memory reclamation, and
a block index (\texttt{block}) mapping to the KV store.

To integrate with PagedAttention, only block-aligned prefixes are
treated as valid cache entries, ensuring consistency with the
underlying paged memory manager.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{subfigure}[t]{0.49\linewidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,height=0.22\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../figures/4.1.a.png}
  \end{subfigure}\hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[t]{0.49\linewidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,height=0.22\textheight,keepaspectratio]{../figures/4.1.b.png}
  \end{subfigure}
  \caption{KV cache prefix trees: local (left) and global (right).}
  \Description{KV cache prefix trees: local tree (left) and global tree
    (right).}
  \label{fig:tree-cache-total}
\end{figure}

The \emph{global KV cache prefix tree}, maintained by the global
scheduler, records cache states across all LLM instances. Its structure
mirrors the local tree, with the sole difference that each node stores
a mapping from tree nodes to LLM instances (\texttt{replica}). When a
user request arrives, the global scheduler can quickly locate matching
instances through this tree, enabling cross-instance KV cache reuse.

\subsection{Agent Workflow Support}

Since Vidur cannot directly simulate complex Agent workflow logic, we
extend it with a \texttt{UnifiedRequest} class that inherits from
\texttt{Request} and adds \texttt{agent\_type} and \texttt{agent\_id}
metadata for workflow identification.

\texttt{UnifiedRequest} further encodes a \emph{workflow graph} of
call-chain dependencies: subsequent stage requests are activated only
after all predecessor requests in the graph have completed, faithfully
simulating serial and parallel multi-step Agent interactions.


\section{Evaluation}\label{sec:evaluation}

\subsection{Experimental Setup}

\subsubsection{Hardware and Software Environment}
All model training and experimental evaluation are conducted on a
physical server running Ubuntu 18.04.6 LTS, equipped with dual Intel
Xeon E5-2680 v3 processors (2.50\,GHz) and two NVIDIA GeForce RTX
3090 GPUs.

For inference simulation we use the Vidur simulator. We emulate a
four node LLM inference cluster in which each node is equipped with a
40\,GB NVIDIA A100 GPU and independently deploys one
Meta-Llama-3-8B instance by default. The prefill chunk size (\texttt{chunk\_size})
is set to 4096 and the maximum batch capacity (\texttt{batch\_size})
to 512.

Agent workflows are implemented with the LangChain
framework~\cite{langchain} to generate realistic request sequences and
workflow topologies for the experiments.

\subsubsection{RL Training Configuration}
We set the learning rates for offline pretraining and online fine-tuning to $5\times10^{-5}$ 
and $1\times10^{-5}$, respectively, with a discount factor $\gamma = 0.995$. 
The experience replay buffer is configured with a maximum capacity of 50,000 samples 
and a warm-up period of 500 samples. During training, the policy network, 
which features a hidden dimension of 512, is optimized using mini-batches of 256 samples. 
To ensure stability, the target network is updated every 100 steps, while the 
$\varepsilon$-greedy exploration rate is linearly decayed from 0.5 to 0.01.

For DynA, we set the look-ahead window $\Delta=1$, the anti-starvation
threshold $\tau_{\max}=10.0$\,s, and $\eta=0.1$.

\subsubsection{Agent Workloads}
To comprehensively evaluate the scheduling framework across diverse Agent topologies, 
we curate three representative LLM workloads featuring sequential, parallel, 
and hybrid execution graphs. In our evaluation traces, workflows follow a Poisson arrival 
process to emulate realistic varying loads.
Figure~\ref{fig:four-images-total} shows the token-length and
request-count distributions of each workload.

\textbf{ChatBot (serial).}
Derived from the ShareGPT dataset~\cite{chiang2023vicuna}, we build $\sim$8,000 multi-turn 
dialogue workflows (2--20 rounds each). Context lengths follow a right-skewed distribution
 (mean 837.54 tokens, concentrated under 2,000), while generation lengths average 191.78 
 tokens, mirroring standard long-context/short-generation conversational patterns.

\textbf{Map-Reduce Summarization (parallel).}
This Agent utilizes a Map phase to concurrently summarize document chunks and a Reduce 
phase for final aggregation. Using Wikidata~\cite{vrandevcic2014wikidata}, we construct 
$\sim$1,000 workflows (2--50 requests each). The average context length is 833.51 tokens 
with a higher variance than the ChatBot, alongside an average generation length of 186.51 
tokens.

\textbf{Code Generation Agent (hybrid).}
We formulate a multi-stage pipeline (User $\to$ Planner $\to$ Workers $\to$ Evaluators 
$\to$ Verifier) with a feedback loop that triggers iterative repair upon evaluation failure.
 Using HumanEval~\cite{chen2021evaluating} and MBPP~\cite{austin2021program}, we extract 
 $\sim$1,000 workflows (2--50 requests each). Because iterative repairs accumulate 
 extensive history, the mean context length spikes to 3541.76 tokens, and a mean generation
  length of 800.87 tokens.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{subfigure}[t]{0.23\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/5.2.a.pdf}
    \caption{Token Distribution of ShareGPT Dataset}
    \label{fig:top-left}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[t]{0.23\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/5.2.b.pdf}
    \caption{Token Distribution of Wikidata Dataset}
    \label{fig:top-right}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[t]{0.23\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/5.2.c.pdf}
    \caption{Token Distribution of CodeAgent Dataset}
    \label{fig:bottom-left}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[t]{0.23\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../figures/5.2.d.pdf}
    \caption{Request Count Distribution of Different Workflows}
    \label{fig:bottom-right}
  \end{subfigure}
  \caption{Feature Distributions of Different Agent Workflows}
  \label{fig:four-images-total}
\end{figure*}

%Evaluation proceeds in two steps: first, each of the three workloads
%is tested in isolation; then a mixed workload combining all three types
%in preset ratios is evaluated. Workflow inter-arrival times follow a
%Poisson distribution. All experiments use the Llama3-8B model by
%default.

\subsection{Baselines}

We compare RAGS against the following baselines.

\textbf{Round-Robin.}
Serving as our naive baseline, this strategy pairs First-Come-First-Serve (FCFS) 
queuing with round-robin routing. It treats all inference requests independently, 
operating entirely without Agent workflow awareness.

\textbf{Parrot~\cite{lin2024parrot}.}
A state-of-the-art LLM serving system that uses semantic variable abstraction to expose
inter-request DAG dependencies to the serving layer.
It employs session affinity routing to maximize KV-cache prefix reuse while aggregating
similar requests to satisfy application-level performance goals.
We faithfully replicate its FCFS queuing and session affinity routing algorithms in our evaluation.

\textbf{Ayo~\cite{tan2025towards}.}
A fine-grained LLM orchestration framework that prioritizes requests based on their
topological depth in the workflow DAG, with round-robin routing.
We isolate and implement only its topology-aware queuing and routing logic for comparison.

\subsection{Main Results}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{../figures/5.3.pdf}
  \caption{End-to-end performance comparison under three single-type
    workloads at varying request rates.}
  \label{fig:exp-e2e-latency}
\end{figure*}

Figure~\ref{fig:exp-e2e-latency} presents the end-to-end (E2E) performance of 
each method under the three single-type workloads across varying arrival rates.

\textbf{ChatBot (Sequential).}
At an arrival rate of 20 workflows/s, RAGS achieves up to a 5.2x reduction 
in mean E2E latency compared to Round-Robin and Ayo. Under high concurrency, 
Round-Robin's P50 TTFT degrades to 17 s, whereas RAGS maintains it below 1 s via 
RL-driven load balancing and DynA's priority queuing. Compared to Parrot, RAGS 
yields a marginal 1.1x speedup. Because ChatBot features a fixed topology with 
regular KV cache sharing, Parrot's static prefix routing already exploits cache 
locality effectively, leaving limited headroom for dynamic routing improvements.

\textbf{Map-Reduce Summarization (Parallel).}
Because this workflow exhibits a simple two-level topology, performance gains are moderate. 
At 20 workflows/s, RAGS reduces mean E2E latency by roughly 6\% over Round-Robin. 
Ayo's topology-based prioritization degenerates to basic round-robin because it cannot 
differentiate among same-level Map nodes. In contrast, DynA tracks actual workflow progress 
to prioritize requests nearing the Reduce aggregation, effectively alleviating 
synchronization bottlenecks.

\textbf{Code Generation Agent (Hybrid).}
This workload incorporates autonomous repair loops to evaluate scheduling under dynamic 
topologies. Here, Parrot's static session affinity induces global load imbalances and queue
 congestion, resulting in a mean E2E latency of 240 s and a P95 tail of 600 s (roughly 
 3--4x higher than RAGS). By integrating RL-driven load balancing with DynA's 
 progress-driven queuing, RAGS circumvents these overloaded bottlenecks. Consequently, 
 it stabilizes mean E2E latency between 70--85 s and maintains a P95 latency around 200 s 
 at 5 workflows/s.

 TTFT metrics further highlight queue congestion under dynamic loads. At 5 workflows/s, 
 Parrot's P50 TTFT degrades to approximately 6 s. By jointly optimizing KV cache reuse and 
 real-time load distribution, the holistic RAGS framework restricts P50 TTFT to under 0.5 s,
  demonstrating its resilience against dynamic workflow fluctuations.

\textbf{Mixed workload.}
Production environments inherently process heterogeneous workflow mixtures. 
To evaluate system robustness under such conditions, we synthesize a dataset of 
500 workflows, varying the composition ratio $(x, y, z)$ of the ChatBot, Summarization, 
and Code Agent workloads at a constant arrival rate of 5 workflows/s. 
Figure~\ref{fig:exp-e2e-latency-mix} details the mean E2E latency across four 
representative mixture ratios.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{../figures/5.4.pdf}
  \caption{End-to-end performance comparison under mixed loads.}
  \label{fig:exp-e2e-latency-mix}
\end{figure}

Without Code Agent tasks (ratio $(0.5, 0.5, 0)$), baselines yield similar 
latencies (11--14\,s), with RAGS achieving the optimal 10.6\,s and Parrot 11.4\,s. 
However, introducing Code Agent workflows (e.g., $(0, 0.5, 0.5)$ and $(0.5, 0, 0.5)$) 
degrades Parrot to $\approx$40\,s, exposing static binding's inability to manage high 
compute density. Ayo and Round-Robin suffer similarly, proving static prioritization 
inadequate for heterogeneous traffic. Under the balanced mix $(0.33, 0.34, 0.33)$, RAGS 
achieves 18.7\,s---outperforming Ayo by 14.5\% and Parrot by 28.6\%---and consistently 
bounds mean E2E latency below 22.8\,s across all scenarios incorporating the Code Agent.

\subsection{Hyperparameter Sensitivity}

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{../figures/5.5.pdf}
  \caption{Effect of bin width on mean E2E latency.}
  \label{fig:ablation-1}
\end{figure}

Figure~\ref{fig:ablation-1} illustrates the impact of the load histogram's bin width 
on the mean E2E latency. Excessively narrow bin width results in sparse state representations, 
which hinders the convergence of the RL algorithm. Conversely, an overly broad bin width obscures 
fine-grained load variations across different instances. Empirical results indicate that a bin 
width of 64 strikes the optimal trade-off between representation fidelity and learning efficiency, 
serving as our default configuration.

\subsection{Ablation Studies}

To quantify the individual contributions of the queuing and routing
components, we run an ablation on the balanced mixed workload (ratio 0.33:0.34:0.33) 
across varying arrival rates, comparing three configurations:
(1)~\textbf{RAGS}: Full system (DynA + RL routing);
(2)~\textbf{RAGS w/o DynA}: FCFS queuing + RL routing;
(3)~\textbf{RAGS w/o RL}: DynA queuing + Round-Robin routing.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{../figures/5.6.pdf}
  \caption{Component ablation under mixed workload.}
  \label{fig:ablation-2}
\end{figure}

As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ablation-2}, performance differences
remain marginal at low arrival rates (RAGS: 11.206\,s, RAGS w/o DynA:
11.205\,s, RAGS w/o RL: 12.867\,s) but widen significantly under heavy
load. At the peak arrival rate, RAGS achieves 104.557\,s, whereas removing 
DynA or RL routing degrades latency to 110.804\,s and 148.758\,s, respectively.

These results lead to three key insights. First, RL routing acts as the dominant contributor; 
omitting it increases peak mean latency by 42.27\%, demonstrating that its benefits scale 
with system load.
Second, DynA provides substantial gains at medium arrival rates, where its removal 
increases latency by 14.8\% (20.782,s vs. 18.104,s), confirming that progress-aware 
prioritization effectively mitigates head-of-line blocking.
Third, the components are complementary: DynA accelerates critical requests into the 
routing stage, while the global RL policy prevents them from suffering secondary queuing 
within backend LLM engines.

\subsection{Heterogeneous Cluster Evaluation}
\label{subsec:chap4-heterogeneous}

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{../figures/5.7.pdf}
  \caption{Heterogeneous GPU cluster evaluation results.}
  \label{fig:heterogeneous-comparison}
\end{figure}

To validate RAGS on a heterogeneous GPU cluster, we conduct experiments using a 4-GPU 
setup comprising two NVIDIA A100s and two NVIDIA H100s, with meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf 
deployed across all instances. Because the A100 and H100 exhibit significant disparities 
in compute throughput and memory bandwidth, the system requires hardware-aware routing to 
prevent unbalanced allocations and resource bottlenecks.

Figure~\ref{fig:heterogeneous-comparison} illustrates the mean E2E latency across arrival 
rates (3--7 workflows/s). Round-Robin ignores hardware heterogeneity and distributes 
requests evenly, rendering the weaker A100s as system bottlenecks and degrading latency 
to over 80,s under high concurrency. Similarly, Parrot's hardware-agnostic static binding 
frequently overloads the A100 instances with compute-intensive requests.

In contrast, RAGS consistently achieves the lowest E2E latency. By jointly encoding GPU 
types and real-time instance loads within the RL state space, the routing policy implicitly
 senses hardware capacities and adaptively balances global traffic. At the peak rate of 7 
 workflows/s, RAGS reduces mean E2E latency by 30\%--40\% compared to both Round-Robin and 
 Parrot, confirming its robust generalizability in heterogeneous inference environments.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related-work}

\textbf{LLM Inference Engines.}
vLLM~\cite{kwon2023efficient} introduces PagedAttention to manage KV cache
with OS-style virtual memory paging, significantly reducing memory fragmentation.
 SGLang~\cite{zheng2024sglang} proposes RadixAttention, 
indexing historical KV caches in a radix tree to enable fine-grained cross-request 
prefix reuse and cache-aware routing.
Sarathi-Serve~\cite{agrawal2024taming} mitigates prefill and decode interference via 
chunked prefills, while Orca~\cite{yu2022orca} pioneered continuous batching to maximize 
GPU utilization. Although effective for single-turn requests, these systems 
lack mechanisms to model the multi-step workflow dependencies inherent 
in Agent workloads.

\textbf{LLM Agent Serving Systems.}
Parrot~\cite{lin2024parrot} pioneers agent workflow scheduling by introducing semantic 
variables that expose inter-request data dependencies to the serving layer, while 
employing static session affinity to improve KV cache reuse. 
Ayo~\cite{tan2025towards}
decomposes complex applications into primitive-level dataflow graphs
, co-scheduling LLM and non-LLM stages to reduce end-to-end latency. 
Autellix~\cite{luo2025autellix} treats the entire Agent program as the scheduling unit, 
employing multi-level feedback queues based on accumulated critical-path execution time 
to mitigate head-of-line blocking. For caching, KVFlow~\cite{pan2025kvflow} leverages 
reuse-distance estimations for predictive eviction and prefetching. Additionally, HexGen-Flow~\cite{peng2025hexgen} introduces an SLO-driven 
scheduler with urgency-based preemption; yet, its coupling to Text-to-SQL pipelines 
severely limits its generalizability to arbitrary Agent topologies.

\textbf{RL for Cluster Scheduling.}
DeepRM~\cite{mao2016resource} and DL2~\cite{peng2021dl2} demonstrate the
effectiveness of deep RL for resource scheduling in DNN training clusters.
TORTA~\cite{du2025temporal} extends RL to LLM inference by dynamically 
adjusting geographic traffic routing ratios. Unlike
these approaches, RAGS applies RL specifically to Agent workflow routing. 
By explicitly encoding workflow-level dependency states and KV cache residency, 
RAGS achieves an adaptive global routing policy that effectively balances hardware 
load with cache locality.
